title: 58 — Amplification
content: <!-- Speed: Explosion | D5+D13 -->
The Signal Key pulsed once.
Twice.
Then it SCREAMED.

Not a sound — a frequency. Wren felt it in her molars, in her sternum, in the marrow of her bones. Every amplified invisible on the planet felt it at the same instant — a flash of recognition, like a name you'd forgotten suddenly returned. The glitch-network lit up like a nervous system firing for the first time, synapses connecting across continents, across oceans, across data streams that hummed with the proof of their existence.
She pushed.
Not just the Counter-Signal schematics. Not just the ANX-00 program files. She pushed *everything*.
Subject 01: Maria Santos. Age 34. Terminated.
Subject 02: James Park. Age 28. Terminated.
Subject 03: Alexander Chen. Age 26. Erased. Legal death certificate issued. Containment recommended.
Subject 04: Anika Bauer. Age 8. Terminated.
An eight-year-old. They had done this to a child.
The names kept coming. Each one a life. Each one a deliberate erasure. Wren felt their weight in her hands, their names in her throat, and she pushed them out into the light.
Subject 05 through 12.
Subject 13: Wren Walker. Age 24. Status: Active. Progress exceeding projections.

She pushed the trial data. The forced overdoses. The containment protocols. The three-year burial operation ordered by Vance herself. The political contributions to three senators and a governor. The suppressed FDA reports. The clean death certificates that disguised murder.
It poured out of her like blood from a wound.

Floor by floor, the NeuroScale Tower's screens filled with evidence. Scrolling, unstoppable, auto-correcting and being overwritten again by the sheer force of Wren's amplified push. The building became a monument to its own crimes — a glass coffin for the truth it tried to bury.

From the street below, people stopped and stared up at the tower. Every window a confession. Every screen a witness. A woman dropped her groceries. A man in a business suit filmed it on his phone — ironic, capturing evidence of a company that had made evidence disappear.
News helicopters appeared within minutes. Their cameras showed the world what the screens displayed, and the world could not look away.

In an office across town, Marcus Holt watched his computer fill with subject files. He recognized the style of the data — clean, organized, devastating. And somewhere in the back of his mind, he remembered the whiteboard. The cartoon. The things that moved when he wasn't looking. The pen. He opened a new document and began to write.

In a cramped apartment, Mayfair Chen was mid-vlog when her phone buzzed with a notification she couldn't swipe away. NEUROSCALE PHARMACEUTICALS — BREAKING. Her followers started asking questions she couldn't answer. She looked at the camera, and for the first time in her career as a visibility addict, she had nothing to say.
Vance stood in her office and watched.
Her face was lit by the glow of her own exposure — her handwritten notes scrolling past in clinical black and white:
*"Subject 03: Alexander Chen. Erasure complete. Legal death certificate issued."*
*"Subject 04: Anika Bauer. Age 8. Parental consent obtained through subsidiary. Termination authorized."*
*"Subject 07: Petra Kovac. Termination authorized. Body disposal via— "*
Vance looked away.

For the first time since Wren had met her — since she'd sat in that fluorescent office and been handed a pill that would unmake her — Dr. Lena Vance looked old. Not calculated. Not clinical. The mask had slipped, and beneath it was a woman watching her life's work become her epitaph.
Her fingers twitched. Once. Then they went still.

In the Tower's server room, Ash found Wren slumped against a rack. Her nose was bleeding — not a trickle, a steady drip onto the concrete floor. Her hands trembled. The Signal Key sat in her lap, still warm, still pulsing faintly.
He knelt beside her. "Wren."
"They'll see it," she whispered. "Everyone will see it."
"Are you okay?"
She laughed. It came out wet and broken. Blood on her teeth. "No. But it's done."
He helped her stand. She swayed. He caught her — one arm around her waist, her weight against his chest. The glitch-frequency sang between them, harmonizing.
"She's still in the building," he said. Meaning Vance.
"I know."
"Are we going to—"
"No." Wren wiped the blood from her upper lip with the back of her hand. "She's done. She just doesn't know it yet."

Through the server room's reinforced glass, they watched the city below light up. Not with neon — with data. Phone screens flickering with the same evidence. News chyrons scrolling the same names. Social media algorithms unable to suppress what was now too widespread to contain.
The world was seeing them.

Not Wren and Ash themselves — they were still invisible, still ghosts in the machine. But it was seeing what had been done to them. What had been done to Alexander Chen and Anika Bauer and Petra Kovac and eight-year-old Subject 09 and sixty-three-year-old Subject 11 and every single person who had been erased by a pill sold as salvation.
Wren looked up at the screens. Her own file was still scrolling. *Subject 13. Progress exceeding projections.*
She thought about the pill she'd swallowed in the parking lot. The rain on the windshield. The Gen Z irony of *what's the worst that could happen*.
The worst had happened. And she'd survived it. More than survived — she'd turned it into a weapon.
The world was seeing.
And for the first time, that was enough.
